data.title: This Signal Is At Its Strongest Since The Pandemic (And Should Boost The Market)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my bullish view for assets that track major U.S. indices. This article is part of a weekly series starting on November 7, 2024, where I bring valuable insights on macroeconomics and investments to SA readers.

My intention with this article is to analyze the latest economic indicators and connect them with my optimistic view. The probability of further interest rate cuts is high, and in the meantime, GDP grows, and productivity increases. A great combination for strong stock market gains.

A Hot Economy

The American economy is hot. Estimates from the Atlanta Fed GDPNow model previously indicated growth of 2.7%, but now, the tool indicates robust growth of 5.4%. It is a significant variation, especially with expectations of more interest cuts.

In parallel, job creation was 50k, below the expected 70k, combined with downward revisions in October (-68k) and November (-8k). The positive point is the reduction in the unemployment rate, however, this does not seem enough to stop the Fed from cutting more interest rates.

Therefore, the investor is facing a scenario where GDP is strong and the labor market needs stimulus, a perfect combination to create a strong catalyst for the stock market. Maybe that's why foreign investors like me are confident. In the last 12 months, foreign investors have invested more than $700 billion in American stocks; it is simply the largest in history.

Productivity Gains

Now is the time where I share the insight that most caught my attention in my research to create this article. American productivity rose +4.3% in Q3, it is simply the biggest growth in six years and well above expectations.

Therefore, we have a scenario that combines strong growth, increased productivity, and more room for the Fed to cut interest rates. This scenario corroborates my positive view for the S&P 500, whose target is 7787 points by the end of 2026.

Finally, still on productivity, an interesting survey conducted by the FT with 183 economists from China, Europe, the UK and the US indicated positive expectations for the American economy. About 75% of economists believe the United States will extend its productivity lead due to AI, developed capital markets, and low energy costs.

Additionally, 31% of economists believe that the US will increase its productivity, and 48% believe that the US will expand its dominance.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

In my research to create this article, the information below left me very intrigued. The US policy uncertainty index is at extremely high levels, however, the VIX is at relatively low levels. Since both are very correlated, which one will fit?

A big and possible risk to my bullish thesis, at least in the short term, is that the VIX adjusts and rises. This is even the most likely scenario when we analyze the seasonality of volatility in the S&P 500. Historically, the first quarter is the most volatile of the year.

It is worth remembering that there are several events that trigger this increase in volatility. When we analyze it from an international point of view, geopolitical events can be major vectors, but I would say that this volatility could come from the domestic scenario; after all, the American Supreme Court will analyze the validity of the tariffs soon.

We must remember that the most volatile event in 2025 was the announcement of tariffs, so this is a sensitive topic. Additionally, the financial market awaits the name that will replace Jerome Powell as president of the Fed. These two events have the potential to increase volatility and cause share prices to fall.

Another interesting fact for investors to reflect on comes from Omaha. The company owned by one of the greatest investors of all time has been a net seller of shares for 12 consecutive quarters.

Meanwhile, the retail investor has never been more confident in stocks.

The Bottom Line

While many investors worry about geopolitical events, the debt trajectory, inflation, tariffs, the market continues to rise. When I analyze all the data coldly, my conclusion is that the American economy is doing very well and the outlook for the stock market is excellent.

As we saw in this article, investors are faced with high expectations for GDP growth, combined with a high probability of further interest rate cuts to stimulate the labor market. This occurs while there are clear productivity gains in the American economy.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. To date, the scenario remains very favorable for investments in the stock market, as the risk-return ratio remains attractive.

data.title: Unprecedented Decisions, Unprecedented Outcomes: The Cost Of A Political Fed
data.description:
A matter of independence

I still remember when, a couple of years ago, China was considered “uninvestable.” The constant government involvement in the domestic financial markets threatened the independence even of the most important companies, ultimately leading to a massive widespread sell-off. No one wanted to invest in great businesses like Alibaba or Tencent because independence is the backbone of every solid economy. Too much government intervention can cause the opposite of the desired outcome: a massive imbalance that drives investors away.

Yesterday, the Federal prosecutors opened a criminal investigation into Fed Chair Powell, and the accusation has been a $700 million overspending to expand and renew two historical buildings: the Marriner S. Eccles Building and another one on Constitution Avenue. I am neither a real estate expert nor Powell's lawyer, but the timing of this accusation is what investors (and bond vigilantes) are currently focusing on.

Chair Powell has been multiple times criticized by President Trump for his unwillingness to cut interest rates significantly; however, from words, we are getting into facts. It is not a secret that President Trump wants to replace Powell with someone else more prone to ease monetary policy (probably Kevin Hassett), but a legal loophole prevented him from doing so: the US President can’t fire the Fed Chair just because of a policy disagreement.

That’s the whole point of having an independent central bank run by economic experts; otherwise, politics would use monetary policy as a way to get more votes. Needless to say, this short-sighted approach would lead to significant credibility damage over the long run. A central bank must always make decisions in the best interests of the country, even if this means swallowing a bitter medicine (high-interest rates). The 2022 "temporary inflation" was definitely Powell's biggest mistake, but overall, as his mandate is about to expire (May 2026), I believe he did a great job.

Either way, getting back to the main point of the article, in order to fire Chair Powell, severe misconduct is needed, and real estate fraud fits this description. We can’t affirm that Powell is 100% guiltless, but the timing of this accusation is objectively suspicious, as stated by Powell himself:

This new threat is not about my testimony last June or about the renovation of the Federal Reserve buildings. It is not about Congress's oversight role; the Fed through testimony and other public disclosures made every effort to keep Congress informed about the renovation project. Those are pretexts. The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.

What is currently happening right now is pretty serious, and we shouldn’t overlook the risk of a Federal Reserve no longer independent from politics. Powell's final thoughts in the Fed's official document sound like a huge warning for the future of the whole country:

This is about whether the Fed will be able to continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions—or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.

I have served at the Federal Reserve under four administrations, Republicans and Democrats alike. In every case, I have carried out my duties without political fear or favor, focused solely on our mandate of price stability and maximum employment. Public service sometimes requires standing firm in the face of threats. I will continue to do the job the Senate confirmed me to do, with integrity and a commitment to serving the American people.

The historical period we are currently facing is becoming full of unprecedented decisions, and this could lead to unprecedented outcomes, either positive or negative. This is not about supporting Republicans or Democrats; it’s about supporting the entire US economy and its currency; therefore, it goes beyond politics.

A hypothetical Powell’s dismissal would follow another unprecedented event that occurred a couple of months ago when Erika McEntarfer, commissioner of the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), was fired with the accusation of “rigging jobs data to make Republicans and the President look bad.” This event as well has set a noteworthy precedent: a lower incentive to issue bad data.

The tangible consequences on financial markets

Following this news, the US stock market futures are falling by 0.50-0.80%, a smaller reaction than I would have thought. However, a less independent Fed is definitely not a growth catalyst over the mid-long term.

Politicians often aim for an easing monetary policy, and they overlook the inflation risk and the consequent deterioration of the national currency. That’s the underlying mechanism that prevents them from reducing the fiscal deficit: no one would vote in favor of a rising tax or reducing government spending.

High inflation is never welcomed by investors since it crushes real returns, but the stock market is currently distant from pricing in this kind of scenario. Nowadays, it tends to give more credit to current AI developments than geopolitics.

Let’s now take a closer look at more liquid markets like Forex and Bonds. The first one, represented by the US Dollar Index, is reacting with a -0.40% that further fuels a bearish trend started when President Trump took office.

To be fair, that’s exactly what President Trump wants: a weak dollar to enhance exports and reduce the trade deficit. As I discussed in a recent article, this strategy is working, especially against Germany. However, the reasons that are leading the US dollar to be dumped are not related to a specific and documented strategy but due to a lack of trust by foreign investors.

Over the last 12 months, the US Dollar Index dropped 10%, not yet at concerning levels, but the vicious cycle driven by the lack of confidence against the US dollar is not something you can totally control. If the market fully discounts the Fed's loss of independence, nothing stops the US Dollar Index from crashing more than 10%. At some point, a too-weak currency is no longer a positive aspect, in particular for such a huge net importer as the US. In other words, a too-weak dollar could be a catalyst for a rising inflation rate.

Regarding the bond market, what is currently happening is the opposite of the desired outcome. The main government goal is supporting a lower interest rate environment in order to boost the economy and refinance the $10 trillion maturing debt in 2026 without significantly increasing the interest payments; however, the recent approach is not working as planned.

The Fed Funds Rate declined by a fourth since October 2025, but the 10Y US Bond Yield went in the opposite direction. The reason is pretty simple: bond vigilantes can’t be controlled, and they always act in their best interest.

If they sense that a specific country is facing some problems with debt sustainability, central bank independence, and deteriorating relationships with allies, they simply don’t buy the long-term debt since the future seems rather blurry. It doesn’t matter if we are talking about the first economy in the world; money always goes where it is treated the best.

Getting back to the intro of this article, an overly involved government can discourage investors, and I think this is where the US is heading. The market needs to find the proper balance on its own, as interfering with the business cycle/dynamics can be very dangerous. This historical period is setting the groundwork for unprecedented decisions, so be ready for unprecedented outcomes.

data.title: Amkor: A Better Business, But Execution Is Already Priced In
data.description:
Amkor Technology (AMKR) is an outsourced semiconductor assembly and test, or OSAT, headquartered in Arizona. However, increasingly, it is becoming difficult to call Amkor just an OSAT. OSATs typically operate at the end of the silicon value chain. OSATs are easily interchangeable - customers can switch OSATs with limited redesign. OSATs typically compete on costs alone, not on design, and have limited pricing power. Increasingly, Amkor is upending that model for its business. It is creating customer stickiness by coupling with design from the get-go. It is committing capital ahead of wafer volume, which is the opposite of typical OSAT behavior. Its pricing power is now getting tied to its capability, including tooling and IP - not just cost. Its huge expansion in Arizona is supporting these efforts of reinventing the business.

As a result of these developments, its demand is coming not only from a single AI cycle but also from communications, computing, and automotive.

That said, Amkor just made a heavy investment in expansion and is still in the early stages of utilization. While revenue is growing along with utilization, margin is still constrained by a lot of front-loaded depreciation and workforce additions ahead of expected 2026 volumes. While the balance sheet has depth, free cash flow is under pressure due to the high capital deployment.

One other worry is that the market seems to have already priced in these developments, with the stock going up strongly in the last 52 weeks. It is not being priced as a cheap OSAT any longer. It is being priced for its advanced packaging scale, even though that scale is 2 years away from actualization. Considering all that, I view AMKR stock as a Hold today - it is a different, better business, but it still needs patience, so the market’s bullishness worries me.

Amkor’s Planned Expansion

The market’s reaction comes from Amkor’s $7 billion planned investment to expand its Arizona advanced packaging campus. This expansion is driven by demand and will occur in two phases. Phase 1 will be completed in mid-2027, with initial production targeted for early 2028. The campus will have approximately 750,000 square feet of cleanroom space and a workforce of roughly 3,000 employees - clearly an industrial-level effort and not a pilot project.

The facility will package and test TSMC’s US-manufactured wafers. Here, Amkor is not going to be acting as an end-of-cycle contract manufacturer. Management has repeatedly highlighted participation in design, with three-party coordination between the foundry, Amkor, and the end customer. This is not OSAT logic and goes to show how Amkor’s advanced packaging has moved from being a back-end service to becoming an important part of cutting-edge semiconductor supply chains.

Management has also highlighted that while Arizona may be more expensive to run, its packages will be productive enough to lift Amkor’s overall margins. This will be supported by government initiatives, including $400 million in CHIPS Act funding, high automation, and higher pricing. The company will also get investment tax credits that will reduce net production costs. Customers will also prepay and co-invest, further limiting idle time.

Advanced Packaging Demand

Amkor’s latest earnings data show that advanced packaging demand has gone beyond AI. In Q3 2025, total revenue grew 31% sequentially, with strong demand from communications, computing, and automotive. Communications revenue increased 67% sequentially, driven by iOS launches and improving Android demand. Computing revenue grew 23% year over year, with demand coming from both AI and non-AI platforms. Automotive and industrial revenue also went up year over year, given the higher semiconductor content per vehicle, particularly in ADAS and sensor-heavy applications. The breadth of demand is also seen from the fact that no single source hogged the sequential growth volume.

Amkor’s high-density fan-out (HDFO) is where most of this demand is, and not interposer-heavy solutions. Those latter do deliver the highest performance, but in many situations could be an overkill. HDFO offers just enough density for most situations at a lower cost.

Amkor’s HDFO platform is called SWIFT (Silicon Wafer Integrated Fan-Out). It allows chips to be connected more tightly while still keeping the packages smaller and thinner than traditional substrate-based designs. So, you can put multiple chips close together without needing more complex interposer or TSV-based approaches. So, this gives you a good balance between performance and cost and is suitable for a wide range of products. That makes SWIFT suitable for a number of end markets.

Amkor is already producing various HDFO/SWIFT products. A good portion of recent investments is directed towards fan-out tooling, stepper-based photo imaging, and advanced RDL capability rather than commodity assembly capacity. These packages have more value per unit and benefit from higher volumes, even though they carry higher material content and need tighter process manufacturing. The picture becomes clear from the above discussion: Amkor’s current growth comes from advanced packaging demand, not just OSAT. This uses fan-out physics, including density, routing, and integration. The focus is not so much on simple assembly volume but on superior technology. This makes the business stronger; the technology is better, but it only pays off if Amkor is able to execute.

Market Perception

Amkor’s $7 billion planned investment in Arizona seems aggressive given the company’s market cap of ~$13 billion. However, the actual funding exposure is much less - one reason the market became bullish. They already have $400 million of CHIPS funding, plus ~35% US investment tax credit. Plus, there’s the prepayment and co-investment; all of these taken together could offset the total project cost by $3 billion. To reduce US labor costs, the facility itself will be designed with a lot of automation. Thus, the actual exposure is more like $4 billion - still a lot for the company’s size but smartly supported by external funding.

This de-risking is another reason Amkor is no longer valued as a cheap OSAT. It has had a strong run this past year, and the strength reflects genuine business improvements - not speculation. They have record revenue levels through advanced packaging, operating leverage is better, and net leverage remains manageable at ~1.7x EBITDA despite higher investments. There is even a slight increase in its dividend - although it is still negligible. But the implication is clear: Amkor has transitioned from a cheap subcontractor into a higher-quality industrial technology company. It is embedded into the semiconductor supply chain. Upside from here depends on execution - an admittedly slow process. This is the only reason why, despite my appreciation for the business improvement, I consider it a Hold because I believe much of the upside is baked into the near 300% improvement seen in the stock in the last 52 weeks.

Financials

Amkor’s recent financial performance shows a company in a transition phase, not one already earning revenue from its investments. There is clear momentum, but there are hurdles too.

In Q3 2025, Amkor reported $1.99 billion in revenue, a 31% sequential growth and 7% year-over-year growth, beating management’s guidance. EPS of $0.51 also beat expectations. There was not much margin expansion, but mostly volume growth and disciplined execution.

Earlier, we discussed the broad growth across end markets. However, profitability remained constrained, with a gross margin of 14.3% partly offset by higher material content and new investments. However, as management noted (at the UBS Global Technology and AI Conference 2025), the company has “line of sight and that growth and that scale is going to come in '26.” Current margin pressure is only due to front-loaded investments.

At the end of Q3, Amkor had $2.1 billion in cash and short-term investments and $3.2 billion in total liquidity. So, they do have enough cash to fund their projects without financial stress. Bottom line, this is a longer-term growth story, and we are in the early phases of it. I just think the market has rewarded the company too quickly for potential future growth, so I would like to wait to see how it goes.

Valuation

Amkor’s current enterprise value and market cap are almost the same, reflecting a nearly net-cash balance sheet offsetting the $2.06 billion of total debt. This gives Amkor the flexibility to fund its expansion but makes the stock expensive at the same time.

Amkor is also trading at elevated near-term multiples, with a P/E ratio of 42x, which goes down to 31x and 22x in 2026 and 2027, respectively, indicating that the street expects profits to recover. This also means that the market is not pricing AMKR on its current earnings power but on its potential to successfully utilize its advanced packaging capacity and recover margins beginning this year.

The same forward optimism can be seen in enterprise-value metrics. For example, AMKR trades at around 12x EBITDA on both a trailing and forward basis, which is below the sector median but more than double its own 5-year average. Again, EV/Sales of ~2.0x looks reasonable compared to peers but is also much above Amkor’s own historical norm, showing the market’s new perspective on the company.

It is interesting to understand the reason for the divergence between earnings and EV multiples. It means the market is being bullish on a scenario where Amkor will be able to normalize today’s depressed margins, so EBITDA will expand materially. In other words, valuation already assumes a lot, baking in front-loaded depreciation, labor built-out, and other headwinds.

My problem with this valuation story is that it leaves a limited margin of error. If AMKR does well, it will justify the valuation it currently has; if it falters, there is little to support this valuation at current levels. This may make it reasonable to hold through execution, but without clear evidence that margins will get better and cash will be generated, there is little incentive for new positions.

Peer Comparison

In the global OSAT landscape, Amkor is positioned somewhere in the middle, but with a differentiated technology mix and geographic advantages. At the top of the pyramid sits ASE Technology Holding Co., Ltd. (ASX) with a ~$37 billion market cap and unmatched scale across advanced packaging, testing, and deep customer access. By sheer size, ASX is able to absorb demand better than most peers. However, that same scale makes it less able to optimize additional capacity, especially when it is added merely to defend its market position.

There are very few other pure OSATs trading in the US. Names like Kulicke & Soffa Industries, Inc. (KLIC), Cohu, Inc. (COHU), and Photronics, Inc. (PLAB) are all part of the supply ecosystem, but not pure OSATs. Indeed, even ASX, although listed in the US, is based out of Taiwan. Other second-tier regional players include names like JCET Group and Tongfu Microelectronics. These companies are pure OSATs that compete aggressively on cost and volume but do not typically have the right sort of IP for advanced packaging - an area where Amkor is moving into. It sits between these groups, neither matching ASX’s scale nor competing primarily on costs like some of the China-focused peers. Its differentiation lies in its advanced packaging depth (fan-out, flip-chip, system-in-package), balanced exposure to multiple end markets, and geographic diversification, especially as related to its US presence.

Risks

Amkor’s key risk is execution. To improve margins, they need to successfully absorb their additional capacity through proper utilization. They have both front-loaded investment and material-heavy new production, so execution needs to be very carefully done.

The upcoming Arizona expansion introduces a timing risk. Their production is not expected until 2028, so there’s a multi-year gap between capital deployment and its contribution to revenue. This will pressure free cash flow in the quarters ahead.

For me, however, the main risk is that the market has discounted all these hurdles by a small factor, and just last year, the stock went up tremendously. Just like the company has front-loaded its capital, the market seems to have front-loaded its valuation. Like I noted before, this leaves very little margin of error.

Conclusion

From the above discussion, we see that Amkor is materially a much stronger business now - well-differentiated from its lesser peers and more tactically advantageous than its larger peers.

However, the investment case has to build on follow-through, and this is not a situation I am comfortable with. I mean, I am fine with a follow-through on potential, but when the market bakes in all that two three years before the new capital can generate revenue, I start worrying.

This is the main reason I consider AMKR a Hold at this time. If there is a meaningful fall in stock price without a simultaneous fall in fundamentals, I may find it more attractive. But right now, I think its future potential is already baked into the stock price.

data.title: PepsiCo: It's Still A Mess, But It's A Much Cheaper Mess Now (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Back in April, I gave PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) a Sell rating, arguing that the company’s operating metrics were deteriorating and that the dividend yield, no matter how celebrated, could not compensate for the capital erosion caused by under-execution. Looking at the results of that rating today, it has played out almost exactly as I said it would. Since then, PepsiCo’s stock has been down approximately 2%, and even when factoring in dividends, the total return sits at about 1%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has surged nearly 30% over the same period.

We did see a positive momentum following the Q2 earnings when the stock surged over 6% in a single day, but back then, I said that it did not resolve PepsiCo's issues overnight.

Looking at that today, the shares are down by roughly 4%, continuing to lag a market that is up double digits in the same timeframe. While I am partly happy with the accuracy of that bearish call, driven by falling operating cash flows to sales and unstable dividend payout ratios, there were many interesting news for PepsiCo lately, so today I want to take another look at this stock.

Now, PEP has arrived at a fascinating point, where this company can either deteriorate further or make a decent turnaround. We now have a valuation that is compressed to 10-year lows, Elliott Management that is demanding some change, with the company agreeing to it. While the structural problems remain at the same place I saw them back in July, I begin to recognize a small chance for a change.

The Valuation and the Activist

The primary driver for my shift from Sell to Hold is not an improvement in business quality, but rather a reset in expectations. When I opened a price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio on a forward basis, I was surprised to see that PepsiCo is currently trading around 16.3x, which is almost the lowest valuation level we have seen in the last decade. For me, it means that the market has effectively priced in a significant amount of pessimism. While I would not classify the stock as deeply undervalued, it is certainly cheap relative to its own historical trading range of the last ten years.

This low valuation multiple alone would not make me revisit my conclusion about this company if it were not combined with the enormous pressure Elliot has put on PepsiCo. In September, we got the news that activist Elliott Investment Management had built a $4 billion stake in the company. Since then, the pressure has started with Elliot demanding to cut costs and fix the pricing architecture. During the Q3 2025 earnings call, the CEO of PepsiCo acknowledged the pressure, calling their interaction constructive and collaborative. Later, PepsiCo agreed to cut the costs and lower food prices as a result of that interaction.

So our engagement with Elliott has been -- we had a couple of interactions, very constructive and collaborative, and we're trying to understand each other.

This puts PepsiCo in a unique, albeit difficult, position. It is rare to see a company of this size under such direct activist pressure perform well immediately, as management's views often clash with the activist's demands. However, the fact that management has already agreed to price cuts suggests they are ready for the change, and that has been viewed positively by the market. The shares rose in a couple of trading sessions when the change was discussed, and several analyst firms had upgraded their price targets on PepsiCo as a result. So far, so good.

A Structural Headwind

While the valuation is undeniably cheaper than it was, by their volumes, it looks like things are not moving in PepsiCo’s direction.

In foods, we changed the promo strategy in the summer, and we went rather than very deep on a particular brand as we did in '24. We try to provide everyday low value or better value to -- across all the brands. That impacted the volume, better revenue realization, so probably a more balanced growth of the category and our competitiveness in the category. So that explains a little bit the Q3 volume.

I am beginning to think we may actually see a shift not only for PepsiCo in food volume but throughout the industry, or at least I very much hope we will. This thought is coming due to the new dietary guidance issued by the U.S. administration. While I don’t believe people will actually read it, not to say listen to it, I think it could support the already formed demand for the behavioral switch in the U.S. I notice how a growing, trendy demographic (social media trends, etc.) is aggressively cutting out ultra-processed foods, which is also driven by the rise of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs. PepsiCo is exposed to these shifts. While their beverage division actually grew volume in Q3 (not surprising with their Zero Sugar products), the food business has been flat to negative. If the U.S. consumer gets serious about cutting unnecessary fats and sugars, PepsiCo’s snack division will continue to drag this company down.

I really think the Americans should revisit their food behavior, because it really strikes me to see the ways that are the norm there. For example, I was watching a content creator on Instagram who bakes sourdough bread (a new hobby of mine, which is associated with “healthy”). She decided to make this bread tastier with crunchy onions, so she used a big pack of about 250 grams, or half a pound, of onions fried in palm oil in a single loaf, adding to that some sauce and parmesan cheese. Don’t get me wrong, it could definitely be tasty, but she turned a healthy staple into a cholesterol bomb. What surprised me even more is the fact that nobody in the comments section was as confused as I am. No one even mentioned the amount of unnecessary fat that was used. Although this is probably good news for PepsiCo. Even though things are changing, it happens very slowly.

In the Q3 call, the management also highlighted innovation in "permissible snacks," mentioning high-protein options like Muscle Milk and new "clean label" platforms like a "Naked" line with no artificial colors. I am even curious who will be faster - the U.S. consumer in cutting sugars and fats or PepsiCo in switching to the healthier options?

The Financials

Enough with the personal observations; we are here for the numbers.

Over the last decade, PepsiCo’s revenue has grown by approximately 46%, a compounded annual growth rate of roughly 4.5%. This is not bad growth for such a large company, and this is the growth I would expect from PepsiCo going forward. The problem is, this growth did not come for free.

Their long-term debt has increased by nearly 28% in the same timeframe. Borrowing to fund growth is standard, but only if that growth is efficient, which doesn’t look like PepsiCo's case.

The cash flow from operations (CFO) to sales ratio has been in a decline, falling from highs near 17% down to 12.73% on a trailing twelve-month basis.

Furthermore, the return on invested capital (ROIC) is at just under 11%. When you consider that PepsiCo’s weighted average cost of capital (WACC) is at around 7%, the spread, or the actual value created for every dollar invested, looks very thin for a complex company like this.

Besides, the dividend, for which most of the investors are sticking to PepsiCo, doesn’t look healthy for a substantial period of time. The cash dividend payout ratio for the trailing twelve months has spiked to over 111%. Even on a GAAP basis, the payout ratio is near 100%, much higher than the sector average of 62%. Historically, PepsiCo maintained this ratio closer to 70%. Currently, the company is paying out more than it earns to maintain its Dividend King status, which gives you an unsafe yield. It means the company is borrowing or burning cash to pay shareholders, which hurts the business long-term.

Conclusion

PepsiCo is a company in transition. For the investor who bought this stock in the 1990s or early 2000s, there is little reason to sell now. Your cost basis is likely low, and despite the headwinds, the activist involvement provides a potential floor for the stock price. The valuation is low enough that further downside might be limited compared to the general market.

For new money, I am upgrading my Sell to a Hold. The disconnect between PepsiCo’s performance and the broader market is a reflection of a business model that is struggling to adapt to a modern, efficiency-demanding world. The market has presented incredible opportunities elsewhere, and tying up capital in a company with a 100%+ payout ratio and declining cash flow efficiency feels like unnecessary risk, especially for the dividend investor.

I am upgrading my rating to Hold solely because the valuation has compressed to a point where the bad news is priced in, and Elliott’s involvement could spark a turnaround. But unless PepsiCo can prove it can grow without leveraging its future to pay its dividend, it remains a dinosaur.
